column78: An initial breeding attempt progressed into early Brooding but was classified as Abandoned based on a brief occurrence of recordings containing Begging Calls followed by a rapid decline in Tricolored Blackbird vocal detections. Two additional breeding attempts occurred later in the season, each characterized by a complete sequence of recordings containing Nestling Begging Calls followed by Fledgling Begging Calls. The temporal spacing and partial overlap of these breeding attempts support classification of this colony as Complex. Recordings containing Fledgling Begging Calls were detected for the latter two attempts, and each was therefore classified as Successful. The ARU deployed at this site was an LG cell phone, which produced lower-quality recordings than AudioMoth units and was prone to intermittent failure, resulting in gaps in recording coverage; while recording quality was sufficient for this analysis, direct comparisons to other datasets should be made with caution.
column84: A 0.9-ha, stream-fed pond containing cattails and bulrushes, together covering approximately 60% of the pond. Grassland grazed by cattle surrounds the site.

column78:
Tricolored Blackbird vocalizations were detected immediately following deployment; however, within six days detections declined to fewer than one recording per day containing vocalizations. Female Chatter detections were rare, indicating that sustained breeding effort was not established at this site. Construction activity adjacent to the site was temporally coincident with the deployment and may have contributed to the lack of colony establishment.
The ARU deployed at this site was an LG cell phone, which produced lower-quality recordings than AudioMoth units and was prone to intermittent failure, resulting in gaps in recording coverage. While recording quality was sufficient for this analysis, direct comparisons to other datasets should be made with caution.
column84: A 0.2-ha patch of Himalayan blackberry in a small area designated as open space surrounded by roadways, commercial stores, parking lots, and a housing development. Commercial and residential construction occurred within 80 m of the nesting site in 2018, and construction activities continued during the breeding period.

column78:
Reduced signal clarity and incomplete data due to device failure in the middle of the deployment precludes assignng dates for breeding stages. Detections of Fledgling Begging Calls early in the deployment suggest that the ARU was placed late into the breeding attempt. A manual review of the data revealed Female Chatter overlaping with Begging Calls, indicating an Asynchronous colony structure. The detection of Fledgling Begging Calls indicates that this colony was classified as Successful. The colony was adjacent to an active wetland mitigation construction project; however, construction activities were halted around the colony during the breeding period and did not appear to affect the observed breeding outcome.
The ARU deployed at this site was an LG cell phone, which produced lower-quality recordings than AudioMoth units and was prone to intermittent failure, resulting in gaps in recording coverage. While recording quality was sufficient for this analysis, direct comparisons to other datasets should be made with caution.
column79: Fledgling Begging Calls detected early in the deployment likely originated from an adjacent portion of the colony where fledging had already begun. Later-stage Begging Calls reflected the attempt recorded by this ARU. Despite reduced signal quality and incomplete data due to device failure late in the deployment, the presence of Fledgling Begging Calls confirms successful reproduction. The colony was adjacent to an active wetland mitigation construction project, though construction was halted around the colony during the breeding period and did not appear to affect breeding success.
column84: Several patches of Himalayan blackberry, totaling approximately 0.6 ha, along Markham Ravine Creek. column78:
The ARU was deployed during Incubation. A limited number of Hatchling Begging Call detections were recorded, followed by a rapid cessation of Tricolored Blackbird detections. The brief occurrence of dependent-young vocalizations followed by complete acoustic absence supports classification of this breeding attempt as Abandoned. A small rain event was temporally coincident with the estimated date of hatching; however, the decline in Female Chatter detections preceded the rain event, and the cause of the Abandonment could not be determined from the available data. 
Tricolored Blackbird vocal detections were present in only a small number of recordings, resulting in reduced confidence in the assignment of breeding-stage dates. Dates were assigned based on the first detected vocalization; however, because detections were infrequent, the true onset of breeding stages likely occurred earlier and reported dates should be considered approximate. 
The ARU deployed at this site was an LG cell phone, which produced lower-quality recordings than AudioMoth units and was prone to intermittent failure, resulting in gaps in recording coverage. While recording quality was sufficient for this analysis, direct comparisons to other datasets should be made with caution.
column84: A series of small copses of Himalayan blackberry alongside Yankee Slough. Most of the blackberry is on a private conservation preserve, Yankee Slough Preserve, that is closed to the public and accessible only with permission.

column78:
Female Chatter detections indicative of early breeding activity were present initially, followed by a rapid cessation of Tricolored Blackbird vocal detections. No Begging Call detections from dependent young were recorded. This abrupt decline in detections supports classification of the breeding attempt as Abandoned shortly after initiation.
Helicopter noise was detected acoustically at the site and was temporally coincident with the inferred timing of Abandonment.
The ARU deployed at this site was a Song Meter, which produced high-quality recordings; however, direct comparisons to other datasets should be made with caution.

No Nestling or Fledgling Begging Call detections were recorded following this decline, supporting classification of the breeding attempt as Abandoned.
The timing of Abandonment was temporally coincident with repeated helicopter noise detected acoustically at the site.
The ARU deployed at this site was an LG cell phone, which produced lower-quality recordings than AudioMoth units and was prone to intermittent failure, resulting in gaps in recording coverage. column78: The colony was located approximately 300 m from the ARU, resulting in reduced signal clarity and limiting interpretability of some recordings. Automated pattern matching detected Male Song, Male Chorus, and Fledgling Begging Calls, while detections of Female Chatter and Hatchling and Nestling Begging Calls required manual review to confirm their presence. Manual review did not identify Hatchling Begging Calls, and the onset of Brooding could not be determined directly from the acoustic record. Based on the timing of other detected vocalizations, the onset of Brooding was estimated to have occurred around 12 June. Recordings containing Fledgling Begging Calls were detected, and the breeding attempt was therefore classified as Successful.

column78: There were two bouts of Male Chorus detected, with the first bout being followed by Female Chatter, indicating a bout of Incubation. Approximately 26 days after the cessation of Male Chorus, the first Fledgling Begging Calls are heard, which correlates perfectly with the expected date if Incubation started just after the cessation of the first bout of Male Chorus (31-May). Onset of the second bout of Male Chorus coincides with the expected onset of Brooding (12-Jun). I have noted that many colonies have a burst of Male Chorus right at onset of Brooding.

column78: The ARU was placed during Settlement. column78: Female Chatter, Nestling Begging Call, and Fledgling Begging Call detections overlapped extensively, producing a prolonged interval of mixed-stage detections characteristic of Asynchronous nesting. Recordings containing Fledgling Begging Calls were detected frequently, and the breeding attempt was therefore classified as Successful.

column78: The ARU was placed during Settlement. Recordings were faint for several vocalizations of interest, likely due to the distance between the ARU and the colony. Female Chatter detections declined on 28 May, coincident with a period of extreme heat. Automated pattern matching did not detect early-season Begging Calls; therefore, a manual review was conducted and allowed the onset of Hatchling Begging Calls to be determined. Persistence of Male Chorus into Incubation, together with extended temporal detection of Nestling and Fledgling Begging Calls, indicates an Asynchronous breeding attempt. The presence of recordings containing Fledgling Begging Calls confirms classification of the breeding attempt as Successful.
column80: The ARU was placed distant from the main part of the colony, so most of the vocalizations were very faint. This made evaluation of Male Chorus tricky. A few nests were close to the ARU, allowing for analysis of timing of vocalizations. The earliest vocalizations of both the hatchlings and nestlings were not picked up by the pattern matching jobs, likely due to them being faint in the recordings, but a manual analysis for Hatchling Begging Calls allowed for estimation of hatching date. Fledgling Begging Calls, being much louder, were detected by the pattern matching jobs, allowing for estimation of fledging and dispersal dates.

column78:
Male Song detections were present, but the number of simultaneously singing males was too low to produce the overlapping vocalizations characteristic of a sustained Male Chorus. Female Chatter detections persisted for an extended period prior to the first detections of Hatchling and Nestling Begging Calls. A period of heavy rainfall occurred from 5–7 April during this interval and was temporally coincident with a potential early breeding failure, suggesting abandonment of an initial nesting attempt. In early May, Nestling Begging Call detections were followed by Fledgling Begging Call detections in the expected temporal order, but the extended duration of these detections indicates substantial Asynchronous nesting.
The site had been labeled 2022 Ohlone - Santa Lucia Preserve, but was renamed to align with naming patterns of other sites.

column78: The ARU failed shortly after the onset of Brooding, preventing determination of colony type or final breeding outcome. Automated pattern matching did not detect Hatchling or Nestling Begging Calls; however, manual review identified a limited number of Hatchling Begging Calls, confirming that hatching had occurred. Nestling Begging Calls are typically detected within two days of the onset of Brooding; the presence of Female Chatter detections without corresponding Nestling Begging Call detections suggests that the breeding attempt was Partially Abandoned at the time of ARU failure. Due to the equipment failure, it could not be determined whether abandonment was complete or whether any nestlings subsequently fledged. A period of prolonged extreme heat occurred immediately following hatching, including five consecutive days exceeding 95 °F and two days above 107 °F, and may have contributed to the outcome.

column78: Three ARUs were deployed at Conaway Ranch, and analyses from each produced similar results; the datasets are therefore considered duplicates. This dataset is treated as the core dataset because it produced the greatest number of recordings containing vocalizations of interest. The ARU was placed during Settlement. Breeding-stage vocalizations were clearly detected, including Hatchling Begging Calls followed by Fledgling Begging Calls. The sequence and timing of detections indicate a single synchronous breeding attempt, which successfully produced fledglings.

column80: The ARU was too far from the birds for robust pattern matching analysis. Both Female Chatter and Nestling Begging Call had very few validations, and Hatchling Begging Call had zero. Hatchling Begging Calls were located through manual review of recordings. Completed a mini-manual analysis as well as an intensive manual analysis by listening to six recordings from each day. All results are consistent with each other.
column84: Easement pond on Conaway Ranch south of Willow Slough. Grains are available and were heavily utilized by foraging birds in 2021 on the west side of CR102 at a corral holding sheep and goats that is 2.0 miles SW of the pond.

column78: Only a small number of Male Song detections were present, and no detections of Female Chatter or Begging Calls were recorded. Manual review confirmed these patterns. Together, these detections indicate that birds visited the site but did not initiate breeding during the recording period. Field observations documented nesting activity prior to ARU placement; however, by the time recording began, only a few males remained, consistent with the absence of colony establishment.

column78: The ARU was placed early in the breeding season. Male Song and Male Chorus detections were recorded, but Female Chatter and Begging Call detections were absent. Both automated pattern matching and manual analyses confirm that breeding was not initiated.

column84: Approximately 0.36 ha of Himalayan blackberry in the cattle field and adjacent to Creed Road. Grasslands grazed by cattle surround the site.

column78: The ARU was placed at the onset of Incubation. A single recording with a Hatchling Begging Call was located through manual analysis. All vocalizations ceased and colony Abandoned.
column80: Colony abandoned on 14-May.  Recorder was pulled 20-May for reuse at another site. There were three consecutive days of temperatures above 90 from 11 May to 13 May which could be the reason for abandonment. There is a single recording which has a potential Tricolored Blackbird nestling vocalization, though it is brief, and I am not certain that it is truly a Tricolored Blackbird nestling. Also, there is a Red-winged Blackbird adult in this recording as well, so it could just be a Red-winged Blackbird nestling. ARU was redeployed at Conaway Ranch.
column84: This site has an extensive amount of nesting habitat with water and foraging areas.

column78: The ARU was placed at the onset of Incubation. Recordings containing Hatchling Begging Calls were detected for several days, but the ARU was removed shortly thereafter for redeployment at a larger colony. The limited duration of the deployment precluded determination of whether nestlings survived to fledging.
column80: The ARU was placed right at the end of Male Chorus. Based on the density of vocalizations in the recordings this was a very small colony. The ARU was pulled on the day after the first Hatchling Begging Calls were heard, and there are only 23 recordings found with Hatchling Begging Calls. While this site was largely abandoned, we cannot be certain if the hatchlings heard survived to fledging, or if the site was abandoned altogether. The ARU was redeployed at Conaway Ranch.
column84: Large isolated copse of Himalayan blackberry in open rangeland

column78: The ARU was placed during the transition from Settlement to Incubation. The first breeding attempt exhibited a complete sequence of recordings containing Begging Calls and successfully produced fledglings. A second breeding attempt was detected later in the season; however, the ARU was retrieved shortly after the onset of Brooding, preventing determination of whether this second attempt fledged.
column80: The first bout of breeding had some asynchrony, demonstrated bythe presence of Female Chatter for seven days after the onset of Brooding. There is also evidence of a Replacement bout of breeding, though the amount of Female Chatter is very minimal, and if the breeding attempt existed beyond the date the ARU was pulled, would have resulted in only a small number of nests. The ARU was retrieved on approximately the date this second weak Replacement bout of eggs would have hatched. If the 2nd breeding bout had been productive, I estimate that Incubating started on approximately 30-May (end of Male Chorus), and Brooding started on approximately 10-Jun, and fledging on approximately 24-Jun. column78: The ARU was placed during Incubation. Hatchling, Nestling and Fledgling Begging Calls occurred in the expected temporal order for a Simple synchronous breeding attempt. The extension of Female Chatter during brooding followed by an extension of Nestling Begging Calls indicate that there was some asynchronous nesting. The breeding attempt was classified as Successful based on the presence of Fledgling Begging Calls.
column80: Incubation onset was likely just prior to placement of the ARU, and no Male Chorus was detected. Pattern matching worked very well and even Hatchling Begging Calls were detected.
column84: A complex of Himalayan blackberries, tules, and cattails just north of Hale Road on private property that is owned by a lumber company with a defunct sawmill nearby. Birds may fly to the nearby Staas Dairy for grains stored in the commodity barns.

column78: The ARU was placed early in the season and ceased recording fifteen days after the onset of Brooding. A single recording containing a Fledgling Begging Call was detected prior to battery failure; because the typical age at fledging is approximately fourteen days, this breeding attempt was classified as Successful. Female Chatter detections persisted for at least fifteen days after the onset of Brooding, indicating an Asynchronous colony type.
column80: Recorder battery died shortly after nestlings hatched. This was an asynchronous colony evidenced by the continued female song after first hatching.
column84: A mitigation pond adjacent to the Hay Road Landfill. The colony nested in a 7.0-ha, water-managed pond containing bulrush. covering approximately 25% of the pond. The colony is surrounded by agricultural land and grassland on the eastern, western, and southern borders and a landfill on the northern border. A heavily traveled highway borders the eastern edge.

column78: Recordings containing Female Chatter occurred toward the end of the period of Male Chorus detections, indicating initiation of nest-building activity. However, Female Chatter was detected in four or more recordings on only a single day, which does not meet the criteria for colony establishment (detections in four or more recordings on four separate days) as defined in the Methods. No nestling-stage vocalizations were detected, consistent with the absence of colony establishment
column80: Abandoned, battery ran out on last days so just spot checked on recordings I did have to confirm lack of presence, males were showing up off and on starting in Feb, so I didn't have to do all as edge I cherry picked recordings with the MC present, these are just marked as reviewed-WS. Females only sang for a couple of days before colony abandoned. There were isolated Tricolored Blackbird vocalizations after 4/22/2021, but these were scarce after 4/22.
column84: A 0.2-ha patch of Himalayan blackberry in a small area designated as open space surrounded by roadways, commercial stores, parking lots, and a housing development.

column78: Female Chatter and Begging Call detections were not identified through automated pattern matching; however, manual review detected a limited number of Begging Call detections that persisted long enough to indicate that young successfully left the nest, and the breeding attempt was therefore classified as Successful. Breeding-stage dates were assigned based on the first detected vocalizations; however, because detections were sparse, the true onset of breeding stages likely occurred earlier, and reported dates should be considered approximate. Begging Call detections on 31 May lacked lower-frequency components, consistent with very young hatchlings and indicating that hatching likely occurred only one to two days prior to detection.
column80: Manual, due to the scarceity of vocalizations reported dates are very apporximate. There are more manually analyzed recordings than in the graphs. They are tagged "reviewed-WS". Also called hatch day for the colony with only 2 rec=present due to small size of the colony. There were 2 recordings with Male Chorus that are timed correctly with Onset of Incubation, so I am using these as the dates. With more manual analysis by listening to more recordings I might be able to firm this up, but only do after all others. So few birds there was never a male chorus mid-day. There were a couple of mornings with Male Chorus at 8am.
column84: Natural riparian forest with the Tricolored Blackbird colony located in a patch of mulefat and stinging nettle. The colony is surrounded by red willows in the north and west, and by a pasture to the east. The ARU was placed on a T-post in an opening adjacent to the colony at 2 m high.

column78: Only sporadic Male Song detections were recorded, with no detections of Female Chatter or Begging Calls. Manual review confirmed the absence of breeding activity during the deployment.

column78: Tricolored Blackbird vocal detections were present in only a small number of recordings, resulting in reduced confidence in the assignment of breeding-stage dates. Dates were assigned based on the first detected vocalization; however, because detections were infrequent, the true onset of breeding stages likely occurred earlier and reported dates should be considered approximate. For example, the first Begging Call detections, recorded on 22 May, contained low-frequency components (~3 kHz) characteristic of older nestlings, indicating that hatching had occurred several days prior to detection. The ARI score for this breeding attempt was 0.05, corresponding to classification as Partially Abandoned.
column80: Manual. This colony was extremely small. The dates are extremely rough and based on just a few recordings which contained Tricolored Blackbird vocalizations. Male Chorus was never robust. column78: Female Chatter detections persisted well beyond the onset of Brooding, indicating Asynchronous nest initiation. Two temporally distinct pulses of Fledgling Begging Call detections were recorded, indicating two breeding attempts. The combination of asynchrony and sequential breeding attempts supports classification of this colony as Complex. Recordings containing Fledgling Begging Calls were detected for both attempts, and both were therefore classified as Successful.
column79: Female Chatter persisted well beyond the onset of Brooding, consistent with asynchronous nest initiation. Two distinct pulses of Hatchling, Nestling, and Fledgling Begging Calls were detected, indicating two temporally separated breeding attempts. The fact that this colony exhibited both Asynchrony and a Multiple colony type support classification as Complex. Fledgling Begging Calls confirm successful reproduction in both attempts.

column78: Two overlapping but internally synchronous breeding cohorts were detected. The second cohort initiated breeding approximately three weeks after the first, earlier than expected for a Sequential colony structure and before fledglings from the initial cohort had fully dispersed. The temporal overlap and acoustic separation of the two cohorts support classification of this colony as Additions rather than Sequential. Recordings containing Fledgling Begging Calls were detected for both cohorts, and both breeding attempts were therefore classified as Successful.

column78:
The ARU was deployed during Settlement. column78: The ARU ceased recording shortly before the expected fledging date. Recordings containing Nestling Begging Calls indicate that hatching occurred, but the ARU failed before fledging could be confirmed. As a result, neither colony type nor outcome could be determined. Ratios of recordings containing Nestling Begging Calls to those containing Female Chatter indicate that the breeding attempt was Partially Abandoned prior to ARU failure. A period of excessive heat coincided with the timing of Partial Abandonment and is potentially the cause.
column80: The ARU was placed just at the cessation of Male Chorus. The batteries failed on 4-Jun, just before nestlings would have fledged. Nestlings were approximately ten days old, just slightly before the predicted fledging date, and there is no way to be certain if they successfully fledged.

column78: The ARU was placed during the transition from Settlement to Incubation. Breeding progressed to the onset of Brooding, and Nestling Begging Call detections were recorded. Approximately eight days after the onset of Brooding, Tricolored Blackbird vocal detections ceased, reflected in an ARI score of 0.07. No recordings containing Fledgling Begging Calls were detected, and the breeding attempt was therefore classified as Abandoned. The timing of failure was temporally coincident with an extreme heat event, consistent with abandonment during early Brooding.

There are a couple of Fledgling calls at the very start of the recordings. This suggests that there may have been an earlier pulse that was missed. Extremely rough guess.... estimated hatch for this earlier pulse would have been ~5/15/2021, Fledging day ~5/29/2021, all of which was before the placement of the recorder. Though these sound a little "funny" so might be a different species.
column84: Unit placed on the levee surrounding Bridge St. Pond on the San Jacinto Wildlife Area. To the west is private land with perennial ryegrass hay and alfalfa. To the south is the pond which is surrounded by a narrow band of willows and mulefat, and has a dense growth of cattails and bulrush in the pond itself. Beyond the pond is weedy fallow fields. To the east is Mystic Lake bed, mostly grown up in tall forbs, native and non-native. To the north is SJWA irrigated, weedy fields.
column90: 2021 San Jacinto Wildlife Area - Bridge Street Pond

column78: A colony established and progressed through Incubation and Brooding, as indicated by sustained Male Chorus and Female Chatter detections and the appearance of Hatchling and Nestling Begging Calls. However, the ARU ceased recording before the transition from Brooding to Fledging, and no Fledgling Begging Call detections were found. Because the deployment ended prior to this critical stage, the breeding outcome could not be determined.
column80: Batteries died in recorder before nestlings fledged. Unable to confirm successful fledging.
column84: Mature oak trees in proximity to the colony with some smaller shrubs less than 10 m away from the colony. Besides scattered trees, most of the surrounding vegetation is open grassland.

column78: This deployment is considered a duplicate dataset of 2021 Willey Wetlands 2. This was the most northerly of the units. The ARU was placed prior to the onset of Brooding. This part of the colony had the latest onset of Brooding.
column79:
This deployment is considered a duplicate dataset of 2021 Willey Wetlands 3. 
The ARU was placed at the periphery of an extensive wetland that also hosted a large White-faced Ibis colony. TRBL vocalizations were present but often faint, and female and early nestling calls were partially masked. Despite this, a clear progression from Nestling to Fledgling Begging Calls indicated successful reproduction. A decline in detections on 7 July coincided with dispersal dates observed in adjacent ARUs and suggests that birds near this recorder represented a slightly later portion of the larger colony.
column80: Manually analyzed data. Large Ibis colony very loud. Recorder appears to be a little distant to where the Tricolored Blackbirds are, but I can her the vocalizations. Many are too faint for template analysis, so a mini manual, followed by edge analysis was performed. Recorder was placed after settlement. Somewhere around when they fail you can hear what I believe might be the other part of the colony right as they are about to fledge. I also think I hear a lot of EUST around this time. There were lots of nestlings up until about 7/6-7/7. Then there is just one or two that hang on. I am calling it a failure at the 7/7ish date. Interestingly this is the date that the other two colonies fledged. My take is that this area was a late attempt compared to the other two areas. And that the majority of the nestlings were old enough to join the crowd when the rest of the colony left the area. Template worked marginally for MS and NC and did not work at all for FS.FS couldn't be located using template analysis. Did mark presence of Female Song when reviewing recordings, so last date here is from these manually reviewed recordings. Recorder was placed just before hatching date, so no Male Chorus. Manual analysis.
column84: See 2021 Willey Wetlands 2.

column78: This deployment represents the core dataset for the Willey Wetlands colony, as it produced the highest number of recordings containing Female Chatter and Nestling Begging Call detections. Three ARUs were deployed across a spatially dispersed colony cohabitating with White-faced Ibis. Although the timing of breeding stages varied slightly among the three deployments, stage transitions were generally consistent. This ARU was centrally located and was deployed shortly before the onset of Brooding. No Male Chorus detections were recorded, indicating that chorus activity had likely ceased prior to deployment. Automated pattern matching performed well for the remaining vocalizations despite background noise from nesting White-faced Ibis. Recordings containing Fledgling Begging Calls were detected for approximately two weeks before dispersal, and the breeding attempt was therefore classified as Successful. Based on estimated Brooding onset across all three ARUs, breeding activity appeared to initiate in the southern portion of the site and subsequently spread northward.
column79:
This deployment is considered a duplicate dataset of 2021 Willey Wetlands 3.  
Despite substantial background noise from ibis, all TRBL vocalizations were detected reliably. The ARU captured the onset of Brooding and produced a strong, uninterrupted sequence of Begging Calls through Fledging. This site displayed a typical synchronous nesting attempt.
column84: A perennial wetland that was created for the benefit of Giant Garter Snakes. The ARU faced west, across a wide channel, towards a colony in thick tules and cattails that is also occupied by a very large White-faced Ibis colony, with the Tricolored Blackbirds diffusely spread throughout the area. The ARU was placed on the southern side of this colony where a more dense concentration of Tricolored Blackbirds were observed.

column78:
This deployment is considered a duplicate dataset of 2021 Willey Wetlands 2. This was the most southerly of the units.
Hatchling Begging Calls were detected on the very first day of deployment, indicating that this part of the colony began breeding earlier than at the other two units.
column80: Dispersal day was 7/5-7/6 there were a few nestlings that lingered past this date. I marked 6/21/2021 as last Female Song as I located some during manual analysis of nestling call. I relyed on this because template for Female found very few recordings with Female Song present.
column84: See 2021 Willey Wetlands 2.

column78: Female Chatter detections were followed by a coherent temporal progression of Hatchling, Nestling, and Fledgling Begging Call detections. A mid-deployment data gap occurred during which no recordings were collected; however, manual review of recordings immediately preceding this gap confirmed the presence of Hatchling Begging Calls, allowing the date of Brooding onset to be estimated. Fledgling Begging Call detections declined abruptly following Dispersal, and the breeding attempt was therefore classified as Successful and consistent with a single synchronous breeding attempt.
column80: Recorder was turned off on May 15 and then turned back on on May 22. Luckily we are still able to determine hatch date. There is an odd set of ONC much after fledging day. June 20ish. I suspect this is the roaming band of fledglings that have returned to the area and are hanging out in the habitat.

column78: Two ARUs were deployed at this colony. Analyses from both deployments produced similar results, and the datasets are considered duplicates; this deployment is treated as the core dataset because it produced a greater number of recordings containing Female Chatter and Hatchling Begging Call detections. The ARU was deployed during Settlement. Male Chorus detections extended through Incubation, and Female Chatter detections persisted for several days after the onset of Brooding, patterns that are associated with Asynchronous nesting. However, the distribution of Begging Call detections showed a temporal pattern consistent with a Simple synchronous breeding attempt, and this colony type was assigned. A small number of late-season recordings containing Nestling and Fledgling Begging Calls suggest the presence of some asynchronous nesting. Recordings containing Fledgling Begging Calls were detected, and the breeding attempt was therefore classified as Successful.
column79: A single synchronous nesting attempt was evident from the concentrated timing of Begging Calls. Although Female Chatter and Male Chorus persisted slightly longer than expected for a perfectly synchronous attempt, indicating minor asynchrony within the group, the Begging Call patterns show the colony functioned as a primarily Simple breeding bout. Fledgling Begging Calls confirmed successful reproduction before the end of the deployment.
column80: Asynchronous colony, female song continues for 10 days after nestlings hatch. Because of this there is a long fading trail of nestlng calls. There is a drop inintensity on 5/16/2022 suggestling a group dispersed on 5/15/2022. The "fledgling chorus" begins to fade from this point. But good numbers of recordings with lots of ONC through 5/27/2022. I have picked this latter date as the dispersal date.

column78: This dataset is considered a duplicate of the 2022 Baja Rancho Ciénaga Redonda North.
column79: The acoustic record showed the same general pattern as 2022 Baja 1: Male Chorus extended into early Brooding, and Female Chatter persisted after the onset of Begging Calls, indicating slight asynchrony. Despite these features, the density and timing of Nestling and Fledgling Begging Calls clearly indicate that the majority of birds participated in a single synchronous nesting attempt that fledged young successfully. This dataset is considered to be from the same colony as 2022 Baja 1.
column80: Male Chorus continued until the very first hatchlings are heard. So onset of incubation could not be estimated using Male Chorus. Assuming about 11days for incubation, and hatching day for colony of 4/15, estimate would be 4/5.

Each wetland consists of deeper canals and ponds surrounded by shallower wetlands. The site is managed for water and includes winter overwater prescribed fire, sheep grazing, and mowing/disking.

column78: Automated pattern matching detected a limited number of vocalizations, and Male Chorus was not detected. Manual review confirmed these results. Recordings containing Fledgling Begging Calls were detected, and the breeding attempt was therefore classified as Successful.

column78: Automated pattern matching did not detect any Tricolored Blackbird vocalizations. A subsequent manual review likewise detected no vocalizations.
column80: This was a site that historically has had a colony, but no Tricolored Blackbird vocalizations were detected by the pattern matching jobs. To confirm this, I performed a mini manual analysis of reviewing eight recordings every third day, and this also did not detect any Tricolored Blackbird vocalizations.
column84: Easement pond on Conaway Ranch south of Willow Slough. Grains are available and were heavily utilized by foraging birds in 2021 on the west side of CR102 at a corral holding sheep and goats that is 2.0 miles SW of the pond.

column78: Two ARUs were deployed at this colony. Data from this unit are treated as the core dataset because they produced the greatest number of recordings containing vocalizations of interest. The ARU was placed during Settlement. Recordings containing Hatchling, Nestling, and Fledgling Begging Calls were all well represented, indicating a Simple synchronous colony type. Recordings containing Fledgling Begging Calls confirm successful reproduction.
column80: Based on the density of vocalizations in the recordings, this was a very large colony. All pattern matching jobs worked well. Note, the lack of detections on 20-May are due to the presence of high winds that made detections challenging, and possibly the birds were also less vocal. Note – the GPS coordinates of this unit are the same as for the site named 2022 Conaway North A, and Bob’s audio note indicates that there were two ARUs deployed at this colony. So, this site and 2022 Conaway North A should be considered the same colony. Each site was independently analyzed, and dates of estimation of breeding phases between the two sets of recordings are essentially the same. Bob’s nickname for this site is the “Jungle” due to the use of multiple nesting substrates. NOTE – IT WOULD BE NICE IF I HAD AN APPROXIMATE DISTANCE BETWEEN THESE TWO RECORDERS.
column84: On Conaway Ranch to north of reservoir up windy ranch road; on both sides of irrigation canals in both Himalayan blackberries and willows along canals. Nests also likely in tules in canals.

column78: This ARU is considered a duplicate dataset of 2022 Conaway North. Vocalization patterns and breeding-stage timing closely matched those detected by the companion ARU, confirming successful reproduction.
column80: This is the second ARU that was deployed at this colony. See above for 2022 Conaway North results discussion. Confirm GPS location with Bob.
column84: See 2022 Conaway North.

column78: The ARU was placed at the onset of Incubation. Recordings containing Female Chatter declined sharply midway through Incubation. There was also a sharp decline in Nestling Begging Calls seven days after broofding onset. An ARI score of 0.214, together with recordings containing Fledgling Begging Calls, indicates Partial Abandonment. Elevated temperatures were temporally coincident with the inferred timing of Partial Abandonment.
column80: Based on the density of vocalizations in recordings, there were a small number of nests near this recorder, and there was no robust detection of Nestling or early Fledgling Begging Calls. Interestingly, the density of Female Chatter drops sharply, and I wonder if this suggests that the majority of females chose not to nest at this location and abandoned. There was a large increase in the number of recordings with Fledgling Begging Calls on June 12, so while the pattern matching is spotty, I am certain that the small number of nests close to this ARU successfully fledged young.
column84: Reservoir on Conaway Ranch. Vegetation suitability dependent upon precipitation and timing of water pumping.

column78: The ARU was placed well before Female Chatter detections began. A single breeding attempt was detected, exhibiting a synchronous progression of Female Chatter followed by Hatchling, Nestling, and Fledgling Begging Call detections. Recordings containing Fledgling Begging Calls indicate successful reproduction.
column84: A patch of Himalayan blackberry approximately 0.36 ha in size, in a cattle field and adjacent to Creed Road. Grasslands grazed by cattle surround the site.

column78: Two ARUs were deployed at this site. Data from this unit are treated as the core dataset because they produced the greatest number of recordings containing vocalizations of interest. Male Song and Male Chorus detections were recorded intermittently, but no recordings contained Female Chatter or Hatchling or Nestling Begging Calls. A small number of recordings containing Fledgling Begging Calls likely originated from a recently dispersed creche moving through the area. The scattered detections of Male Song and Male Chorus across the deployment, together with the brief occurrence of Fledgling Begging Calls, suggest that breeding occurred in the vicinity but at a distance beyond effective detection for most breeding-stage vocalizations.
column80: Colony never established. There are two recordings that might have a single Nestling Begging Call which could mean that a bird or two nested close enough to the recorder for Nestling Begging Calls to be detected, and there are the occasional adult vocalizations throughout the recorder deployment, suggesting there might have been a nest or two. Also there appears to be a visit from a traveling set of fledglings on 2-Jun and 3-Jun. One possibility is that the colony formed just distant to the ARUs, and the vocalizations of Female Chatter and Hatchling and Nestling Begging Calls were too faint to be picked up in the recordings. If this is true, and if the Fledgling Begging Calls heard are at the approximate date of Fledging, Brooding would have started approximately 19-May, and Incubation would have started approximately 4-May. Note that the results are very similar to 2022 Foley Ranch B, and these two ARUs are picking up the same colony. I have them as having the same GPS coordinates. Bob, can you please check this?
column84: NEED

column78: This ARU is considered a duplicate dataset of 2022 Foley Ranch A.
column80: Colony never established. Also pick up wandering flock of fledglings on 2-Jun and 6-Jun. See results for 2022 Foley Ranch A.

column78: The ARU was placed well before Female Chatter detections began. Two breeding attempts were detected. The first breeding attempt had an ARI score of 0.184, indicating it was Partially Abandoned. Female Chatter detections were not recorded prior to hatching during the second attempt, possibly due to masking by persistent Male Chorus. Recordings containing Fledgling Begging Calls were detected indicaing Successful breeding.
column80: Colony is all over the place, but I am documenting the 2 pulses that seem the most prevalent. The first pulse isn't large. The Dispersal date was assigned because I noted a couple of recordngs with much higher density of Fledgling calls, so a little spike. I marked the highes t density recording with the tage Fledgling Dispersal. I still need to analyze the second pulse. I believe that there are two asynchronous pulses. The first HD 4/12-4/15, the second HD ~<5/22/-6/1. Or it is a continuously laying colony.
column84: A 7.0-ha, water-managed pond containing tule covering approximately 25% of the pond. The colony is surrounded by agricultural land and grassland on the eastern, western, and southern borders and a landfill on the northern border. A heavily traveled highway borders the eastern edge.

column78: Signal clarity was reduced throughout the deployment, limiting detectability of early-stage vocalizations. Nevertheless, two temporally distinct Brooding periods were evident, separated by an interval consistent with sequential breeding attempts. Although Fledgling Begging Call detections were not recorded for either attempt, the duration and continuity of Nestling Begging Call detections indicate that the first breeding attempt likely was successful and the Fledglings immediately dispersed. The second breeding attempt only had a single Hatchling detected, so the onset of Brooding is not determined. Because of this it is not possibe to determine if Nestling Begging Calls extended for the tweleve days that arerequired to consider the breeding attempt to be successful, there fore it has been marked as Unknown. ADD BREEDING DATES ARE APPROXIMATE WORDING. FOR P2N ONLY A SINGLE HATCHING WAS DETECTED ON HATCHING DAY, AND THERE WAS A BIG DROP IN NBCs ON 6/2. IF HD IS 6/24 THEN THIS IS 10 DAYS, BUT i LIKELY HAVE THINGS OFF DUE TO THE POOR QUALITY. MAYBE CHANGE IT TO UNKNOWN?
column80: Recorder is quite distant from the colony. Likely not getting the fainter vocalizations. There was one extremely faint and small first pulse where I have a few recordongs of older nestlings that I am confident of. Because recordings are so faint and so few I am very uncertain of hatch day for this pulse. I marked p1n,p1f but it is nearly impossible to detect with any confidence, so dates for this pulse are approximate. The second stronger pulse which failed I am marking with p2c, p2n, p2na where P2na documetns the date of abandonment and failure. THe nestlings were about 6 days old at abandonment. BUT - I have recordings with a few nestling calls on 6/6 - so I might be wrong here!!!
column84: Drainage pond beside Highway 68 E at Laguna Seca near the area with the dumpsters.

column78: Nestling Begging Call detections were present in the earliest recordings, indicating that the ARU was placed during Brooding. Hatchling Begging Call detections appeared later in the deployment, indicating initiation of an additional breeding attempt while the ARU was active. Because the first attempt began before recording commenced, the acoustic record cannot distinguish whether the observed structure reflects an Addition to the original attempt or Asynchronous nesting. Recordings containing Fledgling Begging Calls were detected before the end of the deployment, and the breeding activity at this site was therefore classified as Successful.
column79: A colony of Tricolored Blackbirds established at this site and completed a successful breeding attempt in Himalayan blackberry. The colony was moderately large, with an estimated 400–500 birds. Nestling Begging Calls were already present in the first recordings, indicating that one nesting attempt was well underway before the ARU was deployed. Hatchling Begging Calls appeared later in the recording period, showing that an additional nesting attempt occurred while the recorder was active. Because the first attempt began before the ARU was in place, the acoustic record cannot distinguish whether the colony’s structure reflects an Addition to the original attempt or Asynchronous nesting among subgroups. Fledgling Begging Calls developed before the end of the deployment, confirming that the colony produced fledglings successfully.
column80: Recorder placed just shortly before hatching date. Missed Male Chorus though there is some background chorus that occurs on many of the mornings. There are older nestlings in the first recordngs that are overlapping the Female Song and younger nestlings. I believe that we are jumping in to the 2nd round of breeeding. The Fledgling chorus prior t Creche formation was particularly good and synchronized for this colony.
column84: A patch of Himalayan blackberry about 200 sq m that is just uphill from a 900 sq m small pond. The blackberry patch is fenced. Surrounding this area is open grassland with grazing cattle.

column78: The ARU was deployed to monitor Red-winged Blackbirds that were suspected of producing vocalizations similar to Tricolored Blackbird calls. No Tricolored Blackbird vocal detections were recorded at this site. Recordings from this deployment were used to characterize Red-winged Blackbird Female Chatter and to inform interpretation of Tricolored Blackbird Female Chatter at sites where both species were present.
column79: No Tricolored Blackbirds were documented at this site during the recording period. The ARU was deployed to monitor Bicolored Blackbirds that were suspected of producing vocalizations similar to Tricolored Blackbird calls, but no Tricolored Blackbird Male Song, Female Chatter, or Begging Calls were detected. The recordings have not yet been analyzed in detail for Bicolored Blackbirds, but there is no acoustic evidence of a Tricolored Blackbird colony or breeding attempt at this location.
column80: I think this did not have a colony and was placed for bi-colored blackbirds that were thought to sound like Tricolored Blackbirds.... No Tricolored Blackbirds detected.
column84: Cattails around reservoir. Annual grassland around area.

column78: The ARU was deployed for eight days. Nestling Begging Call detections were present at the time of deployment, indicating that breeding had progressed beyond early nesting stages. A single recording containing a Fledgling Begging Call was detected prior to retrieval, and the breeding attempt was therefore classified as Successful. Due to the limited number of recordings and detections, the timing of breeding stages could not be resolved.
column80: Dave also recorded about 25 min of a foraging flock at a church. This was not uploaded to Arbimon, but I have it in the same folder as the rest of these recordings
column84: Small retirement community in the Mojave Desert. Artificial lake with an island in the center, with willows and cottonwoods, lawns, shrubbery. Surrounded by desert scrub on three sides, the fourth side has an irrigated field.

column78: Only a small fraction of recordings during the first two weeks of deployment contained Male Song detections. Manual review confirmed the absence of Female Chatter and Begging Call detections. These acoustic patterns indicate that birds visited the site but that a breeding attempt was not established.
column80: Recorder was set for 1 every 10 min, thus it ran out of battery mid season. A few male song were detected, but no other Tricolored Blackbird vocalizations were detected. So either no colony formed or if the colony did form it was too distant and or too small to be detected by the ARU.
column84: ARU was strapped to a fence post bordering the colony, about 10 feet away from a country road.

column78:
Only a small fraction of recordings included Female Chatter and Begging Call detections, consistent with the small colony documented at this site. Because automated detections were sparse, manual review was conducted. Despite the limited detections, recordings containing Fledgling Begging Calls were detected, and the breeding attempt was therefore classified as Successful. 
Breeding-stage dates were assigned based on the first detected vocalizations; however, because detections were sparse, the true onset of breeding stages likely occurred earlier and reported dates should be considered approximate.
column80: Recorder either ran out of battery or was pulled before nestlings fledged. Colony was too small to generate a male chorus, so incubation onset will need to be estimated at 11 days before hatch day. Unkown if nestlings survived to fledging as recordings stopped when they were only ~8 days old. There is a hint that there may have been an earlier attempt. I have one recording with YNC on 5/16/2022 and there is also a weak FS bout that correlates with this as well as a couple of FCs that imply a fledging date od 5/30. So I am betting that there was actually pulse 1 with hatch day before 5/16 and that these young fledged somewhere around/before 5/30. And there is a second round hatching around 5/27 and these would have fledged after the recordngs stopped. If this is the case, then this suggests a sub colony addition. Unfortunately the recordings re so faint that I cannot be sure.
column84: A marshy creek area with tules on the bank.

column78: Two ARUs were deployed at this colony approximately 100 m apart. This dataset is considered the core dataset because it produced the highest number of recordings containing Female Chatter and Nestling Begging Call detections. The ARU was deployed during the transition from Settlement to Incubation. Recordings containing Fledgling Begging Calls were detected, and the breeding attempt was therefore classified as Successful.

column80: Something about these vocalizations sounds different to me, suggesting that in addition to the MS sounding different in this population, so does the Nestling calls. Note 4/21/2022 1600 there is a human laugh recorded. One recorder has it at 17 sec and the other at 27 sec, so if these are in sync at all, the two recorders are approximately 10X343m apart, or 3,430 meters apart, soooo.... I think that says they aren't in sync. Debi reports that the recorders ar ~100m apart. So, good news recorders agree with date/time/min. Bad news for triangulation they are not in sync at the seconds level. Fledgling dispersal was calculated by scanning thumbnails for a drop in consistent nestling chorus. This was later documented with p1f tag.

The site had been labeled Panoche Creek Preserve Loc4, but was renamed to align with the nearby site in the UC Davis Tricolored Blackbird Portal.
column80: No colony formed. Only a handful of male song heard. Recorder was moved to loc 5.
column84: An emergent marsh dominated by narrow-leaf cattail and bulrush, fed by a narrow stream flowing through riparian woodland dominated by phreatophytic plants including Fremont cottonwood and red willow, and herbaceous understory plants such as salt grass and alkali heath.

column78:
The ARU was initially deployed at Panoche Creek Preserve Location 4 and subsequently relocated to this site. No Male Chorus detections were recorded, indicating that chorus activity had likely ceased prior to deployment. A small number of late Nestling Begging Call detections were recorded, suggesting the presence of a small number of asynchronous nests. Recordings containing Fledgling Begging Calls were detected, and the breeding attempt was therefore classified as Successful.
The site had been labeled Panoche Creek Preserve Loc5, but was renamed to align with the nearby site in the UC Davis Tricolored Blackbird Portal.
column79:
This colony’s breeding type was Simple, with one breeding attempt. The outcome was Successful.
The ARU was placed after Male Chorus had already ended, but the colony’s progression was clear from early Hatchling and Nestling Begging Calls onward. A strong Fledgling pulse confirmed successful reproduction.
column80: Recorder was originally placed at loc 4 and then moved to loc 5. Recordings have been split to reflect their correct locations
column84: An emergent marsh dominated by narrow-leaf cattail and bulrush, fed by a narrow stream flowing through riparian woodland dominated by phreatophytic plants including Fremont cottonwood and red willow, and herbaceous understory plants such as salt grass and alkali heath.

column78: Male Chorus detections persisted for an extended period before the onset of Female Chatter, indicating delayed initiation of Settlement. Female Chatter detections continued for approximately ten days after the onset of Brooding, indicating some asynchrony; however, the dominant detection pattern was consistent with a Simple synchronous breeding attempt. Recordings containing Fledgling Begging Calls were detected, and the breeding attempt was therefore classified as Successful.
column79: Male Chorus persisted for an extended period before Female Chatter began, indicating delayed initiation of nesting. Once nesting commenced, a clean sequence of Begging Calls and a strong Fledging pulse indicated successful reproduction.

column78: Two ARUs were deployed at this colony. This dataset is treated as the core dataset because it produced more recordings containing vocalizations of interest. The ARU was placed during Brooding. Two distinct breeding attempts, indicated by recordings containing Fledgling Begging Calls spaced approximately one month apart, support classification as a Sequential colony. In each attempt, Female Chatter extended into Brooding, indicating Asynchronous nesting. Because the colony exhibited characteristics of more than one colony type, it was classified as Complex. Recordings containing Fledgling Begging Calls confirm that both attempts were Successful.
column80: Nestlings are detected on the very first day that the ARU was placed. This site had continuous Male Chorus, suggesting Asynchronous colony type. However, you can also see that there is also a hint that there is a Multiple bout of breeding in that there are two bouts of fledglings that are spaced approximately 30 days apart. From the density of detections, it appears that the second Multiple bout of breeding was larger than the first bout.
column84: Isolated Himalayan copse on private ranch. Ca. 500 m of blackberries along fence line.  Water available when adjacent fields are flood irrigated, even in drought years.

column78: This ARU is considered a duplicate dataset of 2022 Plumas Arboga 6 East.
column80: The ARU was set for 1 every 10 min. While this ARU was placed in the same colony as 2022 Plumas Arboga East, this recorder picked up a section of the colony that had a higher level of synchrony, though you can see a few Hatchling and Nestling Begging Calls in early May, similar to the Plumas Arboga East site.
column84: See 2022 Plumas Arboga East. This ARU was placed about 250m west of that one.

column2: 2022 Private Site 1 - not real lat lon
column78: The ARU was deployed during Incubation. Female Chatter detections persisted for more than 20 days after the onset of Brooding, producing extended temporal overlap with nestling-stage vocalizations and indicating Asynchronous nest initiation within the colony. Recordings containing Fledgling Begging Calls were detected, and the breeding attempt was therefore classified as Successful. To protect the identity of the private landowner, the GPS coordinates provided in this report are approximate and do not represent the precise breeding location.
column80: Female song continued for more than 20 days after first nestling heard, so this is a very asynchrounous colony. Also hear young nestlings scattered throughout suggesting the lack of synchrony when hatching. There also was no clear cessation of male chorus. Because of this I did not document male chorus. While nestling calls can be heard through 6/1/2022, there is a large drop in density of Nestlng calls starting the 5/26, so I have marked this as the dispersal date for the colony. Tagged one of the last dense recordings with the tag "p1 dispersal". Likely most of the stragglers were abandoned as the YNCs do not continue past 6/1, and they sounded to young the have fledged. But this is pure speculation. IMPORTANT NOTE - I need to obsfucate the location as this was placed on a dairy farm field with equirement that exact location is not published.

column80: Recorder was placed after fisrt nestlings were heard. This was a very asynchronous colony, female song was heard continuously from the date the recorder was placed until 5/24/2022, more than an entire month. Even hear a young nestling on the last days, though only in a few recordings. Also, since this is triticale - rumor has it that the creches of fledflings will hang out for weeks.... column78: Male Chorus detections persisted through much of the deployment, reducing the effectiveness of automated detection of Female Chatter and necessitating manual review. Manual analysis indicated that Female Chatter detections overlapped extensively with Nestling and Fledgling Begging Call detections. This prolonged overlap of reproductive stages supports classification of the colony as Asynchronous. Sustained Fledgling Begging Call detections indicate that the breeding attempt was classified as Successful.
column80: There was a very long tail of scattered female song as well as young nestling calls, so I think there was continuous nesting at this site, though the majority nested together. I have attempted to document this larger pulse with the reported dates. It is possible that there was an asynchronous pulse that wraps up on 5/28, and there is a second weaker asynchronous pulse that accounts for the long tail.
column84: NEED

column78: Male Chorus detections persisted through much of the deployment and overlapped with Nestling Begging Call detections. Because Male Chorus extended into Incubation, Female Chatter detections were masked, and only three recordings containing this vocalization were detected. In addition, numerous Red-winged Blackbird Female Chatter vocalizations were present, and care was taken to avoid potential false positives. The prolonged overlap of Male Chorus with the extended temporal distribution of Nestling Begging Call detections support classification of this colony as Asynchronous. Recordings containing Fledgling Begging Calls were detected, and the breeding attempt was therefore classified as Successful.
column80: Male Chorus was likely missed. There is a constant blur of the colony and other birds of the area that extends all the way past hatch date that I would consider just the background of an occupied colony or sounds of the additional species occupying the marsh. I believe that the increased male chorus was missed and occurred before the recorder was placed. It could also be that the male chorus is indistinguisable from the background of the marsh. It is also possible that the colony was not synchrouous and there was no real end to male chorus. Nestling/Fledgling heard through end of recordings, but drop in calls suggests 6/23/2022 as the dispersal day for the majority of the nestlings.
column84: To the west is private land, perennial ryegrass hay and alfalfa. To the south is the pond which is surrounded by a narrow band of willows and mulefat, and has a dense growth of cattails and bulrush in the pond itself. Beyond the pond is weedy fallow fields. To the east is Mystic Lake bed, mostly grown up in tall forbs, native and non-native. To the north is SJWA irrigated, weedy fields.
column90: 2022 San Jacinto Wildlife Area - Bridge Street Pond

column78:
Prolonged overlap of Male Chorus, Female Chatter, and multiple stages of Begging Call detections produced an extended breeding signal characteristic of Asynchronous nesting. Recordings containing Fledgling Begging Calls were detected, and the breeding attempt was therefore classified as Successful.
The site had been labeled 2022 Ohlone - Santa Lucia Preserve, but was renamed to align with naming patterns of other sites.
column80: This data set needs a complete relook now that I have my head wrapped around subcolony additions, constant nesting, etc. Look at all of the graphs and really rethink what makes up a pulse. Asynchronous colony that had hints of pulses. Tried my best to document the rough dates of these pulses. Since the colony is aysnchronous these are very rough. After initial Male chorus, there is a scattering of male chorus later in the season, these are marked in the mini-manual analysis and I marked p2c and p3c as best I could to capture these. These other pulses are not as sustained as the first, and female song continues throughout this time. So while there may be other pulses within this colony, but they are not clearly definged. Nestling heard at the end, I think it is not a Tricolored Blackbird, I tagged them as "other nestling" and validated as Tricolored Blackbird nestling call not present. This other nestling has a doublet calls, and it seems less fuzzy and more compact than the Tricolored Blackbird nestling call. Might need to review these all again more carefully. I attempted to mark the 3 pulses even though the dates are a bit blurry due to overlapping pulses and lack of synchrony. The first pulse is smaller than the second, and the fledgling chorus is barely mutliple ONC, but I think I have it right. For feldgling/simple call 2 I am marking the dates with the louder full kHtz calls. UPDATE: By adding the Fledgling Call I think I have this teased out well. I expanded the MC, and can see the three bouts. The 2nd bout of Fledglings is quite brief, existing for really only 5/30. Brian noted colony size in email on Aug 23, 2022.

column78: Two temporally distinct breeding attempts were detected, each exhibiting a complete sequence of breeding-stage vocalizations. The first attempt produced a greater number of recordings containing vocal detections than the second. Given fixed recorder placement and a stable colony footprint, the higher detection rate of Nestling Begging Calls in the first attempt suggests greater nestling presence near the recorder relative to the second attempt. Recordings containing Fledgling Begging Calls were detected for both attempts, and both were therefore classified as Successful.

column78: An initial synchronous breeding attempt was identified based on detections of Hatchling, Nestling, and Fledgling Begging Calls in the expected temporal order. Manual analysis for Hatchling Begging Calls located three recordings on 17 May with high kHz vocalizations. No Begging Calls were detected on 18-19 May which corresponed to an increase in Male Chorus which would have masked the vocalizations.  A second breeding attempt was identified using the same criteria, with hatch dates approximately 20 days apart. Because Tricolored Blackbirds typically require at least 30 days between breeding attempts, this spacing indicates that the second attempt reflects the arrival of an additional cohort rather than a repeat attempt by the same birds, resulting in overlapping breeding stages. Recordings containing Fledgling Begging Calls were detected for both cohorts, and each attempt was therefore classified as Successful. (LOST THE MASKING ISSUE, IS IT IMPORTANT TO ADD BACK?)
column80: Sub-colony addition. After the first birds established a larger group appears to have joined them, resulting in overlapping breeding stages.  Based on density of nestling calls the second addition of birds was significanly larger than the first. The hatching date for the colony for pulse 1 is estimated, as the onset of pulse 2 Male Chorus drowns out the nestlings. So dates marked as nestling call not present when there is the loud male chorus are likely just because I can't hear them. When I do finally hear them, it is obvious that they are older nestlings. A few nestlings were heard on 5/17, and with most colonies the majority of hatching happens the day after first nestlings are heard, so I estimate that hatching date is ~5/18. The Fledgling dispersal date of pulse 1 is also an estimate as the calls from pulse 2 nestlings make it hard to determine the exact date. For recordings that were analyzed to determine the edge of hatching and dispersal (p1n, p2n) where the two pulaes overlapped I have marked the YNC with the tag YNC-p2 and the older nestling calls with ONC-p1 to distinguish between the two pulses of nestlings. I picked the p1 dispersal date on the day that seemed to have a lull in the ONC. There is a temporary lull in p2 nestling calls 6/12-6/13. There was also a rain storm on that day which maybe explains the lull. The majority of the nestlings disperse on 7/3/2022 (last day with high volume of NCs), but stragglers can be heard thru the end of the recordings. Battery started running out on July 4th and completely died by July 13th.
column84: Isolated pond/spring are approximately 2 acres in size, likely natural spring plus irrigation water. Some open water but mainly mix of common reed, bulrush, and cattail.

column78: Female Chatter, Hatchling Begging Call, and Fledgling Begging Call detections overlapped in the earliest recordings, indicating that breeding was already underway at the time of ARU deployment. Recordings containing Fledgling Begging Calls declined sharply by late May, consistent with Dispersal and confirming successful reproduction.
column80: Very small colony, needed manual analysis. Hear fledglings at the beginning, so appears that this was placed after a successful first round. These vocalizations are distant to the recorder, and don't quite sound like fledglings. Because of this, I can't be certain that the young from this second pulse successfully fledged, though their vocalizations are heard 13 days after initial hatchling calls are heard, so I suspect that they did.

column78: Recording quality at this site was low, and faint sonograms limited detection of quieter vocalizations such as Female Chatter and Hatchling Begging Calls. However, Male Chorus and Fledgling Begging Calls were consistently detectable and revealed nearly continuous vocal activity with multiple increases in recordings containing Fledgling Begging Calls. This pattern hints at a possible Sequential colony structure, with as many as four breeding attempts. Because early-stage vocalizations were poorly resolved, confidence in distinguishing Sequential from Asynchronous patterns is reduced, and the colony type was assigned as Unknown. The presence of recordings containing Fledgling Begging Calls indicates successful reproduction.
column79:
The ARU didn't pick up the colony very well and all vocalizations are quite faint. Female Chatter is never heard, but there is almost continuous Male Chorus, and I can barely hear the young. Fledgling Begging Calls, being more strident, were picked up by the ARUs, and analysis of the Fledgling Begging Calls suggests there were two successful bouts of breeding, with the first bout starting with a small group which then had a larger Addition. This lead to the presence of Fledling Begging Calls from 1-May to 6-May (weak first bout), 22-May to 5-Jun (stronger Addition), and 21-Jun tp 24-Jun (Repeat). If this is correct, I can estimate the breeding stages as Bout #1: Incubation started ~6-Apr, Brooding ~16-Apr, and confirmed to have Fledged on 1-May and Dispersed 6-May. There was an addition to this first bout, with this addition starting to Incubate ~28-Apr, Brooding ~8-May, and was confirmed to have Fledged on 22-May and these fledglings hung around until 5-Jun. Once this first bout was completed, a Repeat bout was initiated and Incubation began ~27-May, Brooding ~7-Jun, and Fledging on 21-Jun and stuck around until 24-Jun, after which the majority of the Fledgling Begging Calls cease. There is a long tail of occasional Fledgling Begging Calls that extends through 27-Jul. I do not know if these are stragglers finishing up a really late breeding attempt, or if these are just fledglings that are re-visiting the area occasionally. Mini Manual was completed. 

ISSUE #1: None of the other results that I've reviewed are explicit about restating the dates like this. There's Table 4 in Results that have all the same information. It seems like either all of the sites should have this restated, or none should. 
ISSUE #2: The dates here are different than what's in Table 4. e.g. Table 4 has FBC from 24-May to 5-Jun, above it says 22-May to 5-Jun. Above says Incubation started 6-Apr, 28-Apr, and 27-May, but the table says Before, ND, and ND.

Some alternate wording for the first part: 
The ARU captured the colony poorly, and all vocalizations were faint. Female Chatter never occurred in the recordings, but Male Chorus was nearly continuous, and only faint vocalizations from young were audible. Fledgling Begging Calls, being more strident, carried into the recordings, and their timing suggests two successful bouts of breeding. The first bout began with a small group that was followed by a larger Addition.

ISSUE #3: Remove reference to REPEAT BOUT in the final text, replace with MULTIPLE somehow (could just be "second" and not reference the Multiple, since that word is in the first sentence of the stock paragraph.

ISSUE #4: "Breeding Type" in Col BV is "Async" but above mentions "Repeat" aka Multiple.
column80: The recorder didn't pick up the colony very well and all vocalizations are quite faint. FS is never heard, but there is almost continuous chorus, and I can hear the young. I am betting that the young hatch multiple days before I pick them up on the recording. Analysis of the Fledgling Calls suggests there were two successful pulses, with the first pulse starting with a small group and then a larger sub-colony addition. This lead to a Fledling call from 5/1-5/6, 5/22-6/5, and 6/21-6/24. I can estimate the breeding stages as Pulse #1: There was a pulse that started incubating ~4/6, hatched ~4/16, and confirmed to have fledged on 5/1 - and these fledglings hung around for about until 5/6, so hung out for a total of 6 days. Since the recorder was placed on 4/14 I am unable to document the onset of incubation. Hatchlings are too far away to hear until they get a few days old. First nestlings are heard on  There was a sub-colony addition to this first pulse, with this addition starting incubation ~4/28, hatching ~5/8, and was confirmed to have fledged on 5/22 and these fledglings hung around until 6/5, or 15 days. When marking MC - looked for SOLID bands just above and below 2kHz, because of the distance of the chorusing at times, it looks underwhelming compared to other recordings which have Male Song at a closer distance, but they were not a SOLID band. Pulse #2: Once this first pulse was completed, a Replacement Pulse was initiated and incubation began ~5/27, hatching ~6/7, and this pulse fledged on 6/21 and stuck around until 6/24 for a total of 4 days, when the majority of the fledgling calls cease. There is a long tail of occasional fledgling calls that extends through 7/27. I do not know if these are stragglers finishing up a really late breeding attempt, or if these are just fledglings that are re-visiting the area occasionally. Mini Manual was completed.

column78: The ARI score for this breeding attempt was 0.429, corresponding to classification as Partially Abandoned. Elevated temperatures occurred during the onset of Brooding and were temporally coincident with the Partial Abandonment. Recordings containing Fledgling Begging Calls during Incubation in mid-July likely originated from fledglings dispersing from the adjacent site T27-2. Subsequent recordings containing Fledgling Begging Calls approximately twelve days after the onset of Brooding are attributed to this colony and indicate that young successfully left the nest. The breeding attempt at this site began approximately 30 days later than the breeding attempt at T27-2, and it is possible that the same adults nested sequentially at both locations.
column80: It appears that a few fledglings from T27.1 are heard here in T27.1 just after they fledged, confusing analysis. These older birds are not the end of an earlier pulse in T27.1 because there is no evidence of Female Chatter, YNC, or MNC at all in the recordings evn though the recorder was placed with sufficient time to capture these vocalizations if they had been there. Therefore, I am only documenting a single pulse at this location, though because the ON from the other location are around, I need to document this as p2 to tease the two sets of nestlings apart. Additionally, there may be straggler p1 nestlings in the recordings as well, as they don't quite sound like fledglings to me in all of the recordings where I hear ONC when I "shouldn't". Interestingly, I believe that the birds from T27.2 have shifted their second attempt to T27.1 rather than do a replacement pulse in T27.2 and reusing previous nesting areas, thus the marking as p2 would be appropriate. The number of breeding birds is tiny in this pulse. By the end there are just a few straggling nestlings that can be heard to the end of the recordings. I did manage to document at least one fledgling on 7/27/2023 making the Fledgling calls, and I marked these as Simple Call 2 Present. Many of these calls are starting to take on the Flight-call "yip" sound, but their sonograms are much fuzzier and haven't quite developed the crisp sonogram that the adults have. For making summary excel this "second" pulse has been placed in the "first pulse" slot.

column78:
Two ARUs were deployed at this site. This dataset is treated as the core dataset because it produced the greatest number of recordings containing vocalizations of interest. Settlement had begun when the ARU was placed. An initial breeding attempt was identified in April based on the temporal sequence of Male Chorus followed by Female Chatter. A second period of Female Chatter began approximately 14 days later, a separation shorter than the minimum ~30-day interval between breeding attempts in Tricolored Blackbirds, indicating the arrival of an additional cohort and classification of this event as an Addition.

A third period of Male Chorus followed by Female Chatter occurred approximately 30 days after the onset of the initial breeding attempt, consistent with a second breeding attempt by the original cohort. A subsequent, period of Female Chatter in June, occurring approximately 30 days after the onset of the second attempt, is consistent with a second breeding attempt by the added cohort though the number of deections for this putative fourth attempt are sparse. All breeding attempts were classified as Successful based on the presence of Fledgling Begging Calls.

This combination of Additions and Sequential breeding attempts resulted in extended overlap of breeding stages and near-continuous detections of Nestling and Fledgling Begging Calls from 10 May through ARU retrieval on 18 June. Accordingly, this colony was classified as Complex.
column80: This colony had a Complex form of breeding and exhibited both Sub-colony Addition and Replacement forms. After the first bout of breeding commenced Brooding, there was a Sub-colony Addition on 4-May. These new arrivals would have occupied new nesting sites, as the original individuals that formed the colony were still brooding, and thus nests were occupied. In addition, there was a third bout of Male Chorus that occurs approximately at the time the first Fledgling Begging Calls are heard, that suggest that this is a Replacement bout of breeding where the nesting sites previously occupied are reused for another bout of breeding. This Replacement bout is much smaller than the original bout of breeding, and there are only a handful of Female Chatter in June, and only a few Hatchling Begging Calls in June as well. This second weak bout might have also shown a replacement for the sub-colony addition, though it was hard to document, as it was obscured by the vocalizations from the overlapping, and much larger bout of breeding from the first bout, but the presence of Fledgling Begging Calls into mid-June imply that this Replacement bout of at the orignal nesting sites, was also successful. Additionally, there is evidence that there was a Replacement bout of breeding at the Sub-colony Addition sites in that there are hatchlings heard on June 13, which is 28 days after the hatchlings from the first subcolony addition are heard. NOTE - SHOULD ADD THE BLACK ARROWS FOR EACH OF THE HATCHLING BOUTS ONTO THE CHART.

column80: This ARU did not pick up the colony well, and the vocalizations are quite weak. This could be due to either distance to the colony or possibly the unit being placed in the dry-box upside down. I have manually assessed recordings for the presence of the youngest Nestlings, and on 19-May I begin to hear faint vocalizations, so this date is listed as the first day Hatchling Begging Calls are heard. Due to the faint vocalizations, it is likely that the eggs hatched several days prior to this date, and that the first Hatchling Begging Calls were not picked up by the ARU. A second bout of breeding was initiated, though there were very few vocalizations detected. Those that are detected indicate that at least a few birds initiated a second bout of breeding. The recorder was pulled just after the first Hatchling Begging Calls were expected. No Hatchling Begging Calls were detected for this second bout of breeding, but given the nature of the poor quality of the recordings, this is not unexpected. Therefore, I have marked the outcome from this bout of breeding as Unknown.
